details.text: Derivative: جَاءَ means “to come". أَتَى also means “to come”. The Qur’an uses أَتَى for that which is easier than جَاءَ whereas جَاءَ implies a difficult that is not found in أَتَى. As such جَاءَ is used for major and significant things or events that do not occur with ease. The use of نَصْر (Nasr), which is a noun and more emphatic in implication than the verb, implies that the help or victory being implied is major and significant.

Derivative: نَصْر means “help” in origin and was used by the Arabs to say that the rain helped (نَصَرَ) the earth i.e., by bringing it to life. نَصْر is used in Arabic to refer to the help against an oppressor, affliction or army i.e., something that is major and significant. It is used to refer to overcoming an enemy and to the help or assistance that is provided in order to achieve an objective. As such its meaning carries the implication of “victory”.

Derivative: الْفَتْحُ is from فَتَحَ which means “to open” something and is also used to open a land and to gain dominance over its inhabitants i.e., conquest. It is used to refer to something being decided decisively between two sides like a clear victory of one over the other. The achievement of victory is implied in the meaning of فَتْح (Fat’h). الْفَتْحُ “The victory” implies the final and decisive victory i.e., in reference to the conquest of Makkah which signified the end of the struggle between the Messenger ﷺ and the Quraish.

إِذَا جَاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ وَالْفَتْحُ

“When the help of Allah comes and the victory” – The “help of Allah” comes before “the victory” in the verse indicating that “the victory” is the consequence of “Allah’s help” i.e., “the help of Allah” is the only reason for why “the victory” is achieved. Some have understood the وَاو (Waw – وَ) between “the help of Allah” and “the victory” as being explanatory known as عَطْفُ البَيان (a connection made to explain) where what follows the “Waw” explains what precedes it. The meaning would therefore be “When the help of Allah comes i.e., the victory”.

- نَصْرُ اللَّهِ “The help of Allah” – By attributing the “Help and victory” to Allah we understand that it is a help that is incomparable to any other help. It also means that the victory is from Allah and all gratitude should be to Allah alone for the help and victory.

Derivative: أَفْوَاجًا (Afwaaj) is the plural of فَوْجٌ (Fawj). فَوْجٌ (Fawj) refers to a crowd or a group of people and أَفْوَاجًا (Afwaaj) means group after group i.e., in droves and crowds or multitudes followed by multitudes.

Derivative: تَوَّابًا is the hyperbolised form of تائِب Taa’ib). تائِب (Taa’ib) is the one who comes back but تَوَّاب (Tawwaab) means the One who comes back over and over again. Its meaning is to come back over and over again to accept repentance.
Derivative: حَمْد combines the meanings of praise and gratitude.

Derivative: سَبِّحْ is from سَبَّحَ – تَسْبِيح. In respect to Allah تَسْبِيح (Tasbeeh) means to declare His perfection above and beyond any flaws and to maintain that perfection by negating from Him anything that is not fitting for Him. تَسْبِيح (Tasbeeh) has also been used in the Qur’an to refer to the prayer itself.

- “Then (or so) (فَ), extol (سَبِّحْ) with the praise (بِحَمْدِ) of your Lord (رَبِّكَ)” – Its meaning can be understood from the fact that it has been reported that the Messenger of Allah ﷺ often repeated the remembrance of Allah after the revelation of this verse stating: سُبْحانَكَ اللَّهُمَّ وَبِحَمْدِهِ (Subhaanaka Allahumma Wa Bi-Hamdika” and سُبْحانَ الله وَبَحَمْدِهِ (SubhaanAllah Wa Bi-Hamdihi) amongst other similar statements of remembering Allah with these two words in addition to the Du’a for forgiveness included within them. سَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ implies saying سُبْحان الله (SubhaanAllah) and الحَمْدُ لَلَّه (Al-Hamdu Lillah).
- The بِ in بِحَمْدِ can mean “with” according to the meaning of alongside to make the verse mean: “Say Subhaanallah alongside Al-Hamdu Lillah”. Another understanding is that the بِ means “by” with the meaning that “You do the Tasbeeh of Allah (i.e., declare His perfection) by stating the Hamd of Allah or saying Al-Hamdu Lillah). بِحَمْدِ could also imply the way of doing Tasbeeh of Allah and declaring His perfection i.e., it describes the state of declaring Allah’s perfection in that you are praising and showing gratitude to Allah whilst engaged in it. Another way that it has been understood is to perfect the way in which you undertake the Hamd (praise and gratitude) to Allah.
- The use of كَانَ in the past tense implies that Allah has always been تَوَّابًا (accepting of repentance over and over again).
